introSection:
Greezed Lightnin' was a Schwarzkopf launched shuttle roller coaster located at Six Flags Kentucky Kingdom in Louisville, Kentucky, USA.

The ride previously operated at Six Flags Great America and Six Flags Over Georgia. It closed with the park in 2009 and was dismantled in July 2013.

sections.title: History
sections.content:
The ride originally opened as Tidal Wave at Marriott's Great America in Gurnee, Illinois on May 6, 1978. It was built in the Yankee Harbor area of the park. It closed in 1991 and was subsequently removed to make room for Batman The Ride.

It was then stored at Six Flags Over Mid-America from 1992 to 1994.

The ride was later relocated to Six Flags Over Georgia in Austell, Georgia. There, it would be renamed Viper and reopened on April 29, 1995. The coaster was rethemed and featured extensive theming in its queue line including a man-made swamp, landscaping and a crashed jeep. It was the only launched coaster at the park. The attraction closed on September 16, 2001 and was removed to make room for Superman Ultimate Flight. The ride's station remained standing and was used for park storage until 2019. Viper was then moved into storage at the park, remaining throughout the 2002 season.

In November 2002, rumors began circulating that Viper would be rebuilt at Six Flags Kentucky Kingdom in Louisville, Kentucky for the 2003 season. On December 8, 2002, Six Flags confirmed that the ride would reopen as Greezed Lightnin'. Construction was complete by April 2003. To make room for the ride, the Tin Lizzies ride track was shortened.

The ride reopened at Six Flags Kentucky Kingdom on April 25, 2003.

The ride closed in 2009 along with Six Flags Kentucky Kingdom due to Six Flags' bankruptcy.

When the reopening of Kentucky Kingdom was announced in 2014, it was determined that Greezed Lightnin' would be too costly to repair and reopen. The ride was later dismantled and scrapped in July 2013. The station building was reused for Lightning Run, which opened in 2014 for the reopening of Kentucky Kingdom. Components of the ride, including the weight drop launch and tower, remain in storage on the park's property.

At Six Flags Great America, a gravestone displayed during Fright Fest pays tribute to the coaster. It reads "was rebuilt in Kentucky... wasn't so lucky".

sections.title: Design
sections.content: Greezed Lightnin' launched from the station to a top speed of in a total of 6 seconds. Next, the train traveled through a vertical loop and up a 70° incline. At this point, the ride fell backwards through the entire design and past the station to another incline. After climbing and falling down that incline, the train slowly rolled into the station.

sections.title: Color scheme
sections.content: Green track and blue supports. The ride featured sand track and supports when operating at Six Flags Over Georgia, and blue track and supports at Six Flags Great America.

fullText:
Greezed Lightnin' was a Schwarzkopf launched shuttle roller coaster located at Six Flags Kentucky Kingdom in Louisville, Kentucky, USA.

The ride previously operated at Six Flags Great America and Six Flags Over Georgia. It closed with the park in 2009 and was dismantled in July 2013.

The ride originally opened as Tidal Wave at Marriott's Great America in Gurnee, Illinois on May 6, 1978. It was built in the Yankee Harbor area of the park. It closed in 1991 and was subsequently removed to make room for Batman The Ride.

It was then stored at Six Flags Over Mid-America from 1992 to 1994.

The ride was later relocated to Six Flags Over Georgia in Austell, Georgia. There, it would be renamed Viper and reopened on April 29, 1995. The coaster was rethemed and featured extensive theming in its queue line including a man-made swamp, landscaping and a crashed jeep. It was the only launched coaster at the park. The attraction closed on September 16, 2001 and was removed to make room for Superman Ultimate Flight. The ride's station remained standing and was used for park storage until 2019. Viper was then moved into storage at the park, remaining throughout the 2002 season.

In November 2002, rumors began circulating that Viper would be rebuilt at Six Flags Kentucky Kingdom in Louisville, Kentucky for the 2003 season. On December 8, 2002, Six Flags confirmed that the ride would reopen as Greezed Lightnin'. Construction was complete by April 2003. To make room for the ride, the Tin Lizzies ride track was shortened.

The ride reopened at Six Flags Kentucky Kingdom on April 25, 2003.

The ride closed in 2009 along with Six Flags Kentucky Kingdom due to Six Flags' bankruptcy.

When the reopening of Kentucky Kingdom was announced in 2014, it was determined that Greezed Lightnin' would be too costly to repair and reopen. The ride was later dismantled and scrapped in July 2013. The station building was reused for Lightning Run, which opened in 2014 for the reopening of Kentucky Kingdom. Components of the ride, including the weight drop launch and tower, remain in storage on the park's property.

At Six Flags Great America, a gravestone displayed during Fright Fest pays tribute to the coaster. It reads "was rebuilt in Kentucky... wasn't so lucky".

Greezed Lightnin' launched from the station to a top speed of in a total of 6 seconds. Next, the train traveled through a vertical loop and up a 70° incline. At this point, the ride fell backwards through the entire design and past the station to another incline. After climbing and falling down that incline, the train slowly rolled into the station.

Green track and blue supports. The ride featured sand track and supports when operating at Six Flags Over Georgia, and blue track and supports at Six Flags Great America.
